item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Noting that the image appears to be from here, with no indication that it has been released under a license compliant with the wiki's (CC BY-SA 3.0). Thus, it has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one of the best articles I've read in a while, concise and entertaining. I'm hoping it becomes one of the better-known ones now that you've cleaned it up. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agent ███ will receive full compensation.

I expect, this means cyborg eyes?
+1

item.title: Re: SCP-1552
item.content:encoded: I think you're good on the typo fix—it doesn't look like the same edit was made and reverted previously (you can check the "History" section of the page at the bottom right to see previous edits and versions), and someone else fixed a different "SCCP" typo earlier.

item.title: Re: SCP-1552
item.content:encoded:
There was an "SCCP-1552" I fixed. I believe the rules are that you can edit other people's articles to fix unintentional typos, but if I'm mistaken please revert.

This SCP reminds me of The Spectre's ending in Twisted Metal 2. He wished the whole world could see his face and love it, so his face was stretched across the sky. The sky isn't itself reflective, but the reason it's blue is because of light reflecting off of water vapor. If the ocean became an instance of 1552-1, eventually due to condensation, the sky too would also eventually become 1552-1.

Now I want a bulldog to be a playable driver in Twisted Metal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Lovely SCP, just great. Upvoted
SReagan, wherever you are, you should be proud!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow. I'm not sure if this is concerning or cute or what. I mean, bulldogs are adorable, but a bulldog being everywhere? It'd get kinda creepy. This is very well thought-out and just about every possibility was explored. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow. Allright then.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You never heard of remote surgery? This is real.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
he surgeon was able to successfully complete the procedure via a webcam

a webcam

wat

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is on my top ten favorite SCPs list.
The back story to the dog just makes it so damn creepy. Its like, something terrible happens to the dog, and it "punishes" people nearby by forcing them to look at his face for what seems to be no good reason, and still keeps that cute expression while doing so.

Plus its just so damn well written.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ridiculously Photogenic Bulldog.

+1. This is exceptionally well written, with every possibility explored. I didn't even consider the possible effects upon reflective liquids until the article brought it up, which instantly pinned a sudden realization that this could indeed have some devestating effects upon the water supply.

I don't think the superimposed image of a bulldog on every reflective surface in the world would necessarily be a societal showstopper, because humanity can adapt. It's silly and funny to imagine, but in the same thread, I don't think anyone particularly wants it to because it'd be equally horrifying and somewhat frustrating.

Can you imagine if this thing found its way into a hall of mirrors? A hardware store? Heck, AWCY would put it in a glass case in the middle of Times Square.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was reading through this comment and thinking 'What the devil is he going on about? It already says 'incidence', not 'incident'". Then I untwiddled my eyes and realised it should of course read 'instance'. Well done that man! I've updated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What's worse is what would happen when it eventually dies - everyone is now staring at the slowly decomposing remains of a dog, forever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Tiny changes — polishing and cleaning the spelling and grammar, something that will make the original writer say "oh, right! Thanks for catching that!", — can be done without having to ask. It's a wiki, go ahead.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Excellent article. The very idea of having my eyes permanently transformed into live feeds of a dog is both awesome and terrifying at the same time.

I did find this though:

which at some point in the incident also became an incidence of SCP-1552-1.

What are the rules for editing out little mistakes such as this? Does it require permission, or can it be done by anyone?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thankee :)

I certainly didn't notice it - re-uploaded.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a perfect blend of creepy and adorable. +1

Also, am I the only one who noticed the stock footage logo on the dog's nose?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Tarquin was planted so that he'd be taken into Foundation custody specifically to serve as a constant low-level security breach.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is so good.

The idea is quirky and gives a 'not sure if horrifying, silly, or adorable' vibe. And the mechanics and consequences are extremely well thought through. I kept waiting the whole article for the author to drop the ball or just not think things out thoroughly enough and it never happened.

(Also, I thought 'eyes' almost immediately reading the description and sure enough, an eye experiment takes place and goes south in an interesting fashion.)

Very well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's well written. But, it just doesn't do anything for me. I don't find the concept interesting.

Edit: I mean, the dog is even kinda cute.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks - I hadn't actually put that at the time, though :) I've now added most of the above (minus the stuff about the footage being faster than light; I think the concept's complex enough without getting time-travel involved) as a paragraph on its own just before the addenda - tried it out in Addendum 02 or as a new addendum, but it looks odd (especially that this would be suggested after the other addenda which have basically been discovering that 1552 is a pain in the ass security-wise).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: :) That was actually one of the ideas I came up with in a 'research requests' section - unfortunately that section was the first thing to be nixed in workshopping over on the forum. It might actually be a semi-interesting experiment; 093 doesn't take you into the world of the reflection but another dimension altogether, so the main question would be whether 1552-1 is still considered a mirror by 093 (probably not since it absorbs all visible light that hits it like an idealised black body before re-emitting it as footage of 1552). Other hare-brained ideas: using it as containment material for SCP-640, introducing a sample to SCP-944 or SCP-1691 (these latter two requests were to be refused with extreme prejudice). From what I gather this sort of cross-linking's fallen out of favour though; I was warned it came across as name-droppy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Terrible idea number 522: Use SCP-093 on one of these mirrors.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Feels much better now. :D Upvote!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've replaced it with "Subject reacted poorly when informed of the effects of the experiment and attempted to abscond from the facility.", although now it's not clear how they were able to blindside (no pun intended) Foundation staff. I also added a datestamp on the further experiments with liquid 1552-1 so it's clear this is a later discovery that partially alleviates the danger 1552-1 poses.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Our Tarquin, Who Art in our water supply, Hallowed be Thy Name…"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is there any way I can make this more explicit/better?

Hmmm, maybe change "feigned blindness and ran" to "spent a few moments looking shocked, said '[EXPLETIVE] this', and ran".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Aside from the obvious implications for the Masquerade of having a giant bulldog grinning at you from every sink, lake and bathtub.

Heh, but just imagine what all of the end-of-the-world doomsayers would make of that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1.) Same with Guilliman's comment - I should probably update with datestamps to make clear they were initially worried about water supply contamination until further studies were done proving it to be nontoxic and dilutable. It's still possible to convert huge amounts of water into SCP-1552-1, but you'd need to take either 1552 himself or a piece of -1 out of the facility and convert a huge body of water (like an ocean) deliberately. They were concerned that stray molecules of -1 were liable to convert larger bodies of water, which turned out to be an unwarranted concern; it has no inherent ability to contaminate other liquids it's poured into.

2.) Nope; would be interesting but not really in line with what I've established thus far. The subject decided '[REDACTED] this' after having his eyes turned into live feed of a dog and tried to get out, in the process converting loads of surfaces. I imagine him running through the corridors of Sector-25, catching glimpses of his own messed-up eyes in reflective surfaces before each is replaced by the face of 1552 (I considered including the bill he ran up for tiling, light fixtures, etc. having to be ripped out and replaced). In the original draft Agent Censored was Dr Censored, a member of the research team; I simplified that and now we assume he was just the staff member who apprehended the subject. Is there any way I can make this more explicit/better?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At the time that bit was written they weren't sure it was drinkable :) Aside from the obvious implications for the Masquerade of having a giant bulldog grinning at you from every sink, lake and bathtub.

Maybe I should update the 'it's drinkable' bit with a redacted datestamp to make it clear that was a later discovery?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! That could well be a future development for the article, as by all accounts he's just a regular mutt; bulldogs don't live that long either. I had thrown in some hints that 1552-1 might be somewhat autonomous and that Tarquin dying could result in some interesting consequences. Right now the facility is keeping him alive on the basis that 1552-1 is predictable when he's conscious and in good health.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: :( It's a replacement for a hyperbolic rant that was deemed OOC. Would a staff member be allowed to continue his regular duties after partial conversion into an SCP? I can't see he would be allowed home after that, especially given the 'footage of 1552 after containment is now also footage of a Foundation facility' thing. Open to suggestions as to how it might be improved, though I think the Eye Scream is justifiable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
dilution results in the footage becoming fainter before disappearing completely at 1 part SCP-1552-1 to 2 parts water.

If that's true I don't see why the Foundation would be worried about it infecting the world's water supply

Unexpectedly, the subject retained full vision, presumably linked to the black body properties of SCP-1552. The subject initially feigned blindness, before attempting to abscond from the facility. Since the contaminated subject was able to view a reflection of SCP-1552 in any reflective surface they encountered, they were quickly able to spread SCP-1552-1 to the eyes of other D-class subjects and Agent

Wait, so if a person's eyes are infected then the person is compelled to try to spread it as far as possible?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't get why the freshwater supply being infected with this would be such a bad idea

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting seeing the administrator used in an article. Also, this is good, +1 for sure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nicely written. I wonder what will happen when Tarquin dies of old age? Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Tarquin the Proud. I love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
After consultation with O5-██ the decision was made to remove Agent ███ 's eyes. The surgeon was able to successfully complete the procedure via a webcam mechanical remove and Agent ███ will receive full compensation.

this is lame (just give the man sunglasses!), and poorly written.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh my god this SCP is so fucking annoying to contemplate. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That played a significantly larger role in my rambling first draft, but I toned it back :( They're not actually using it yet as a lot of security issues would have to be worked out; at present all but a handful of the -1s are covered in paint and in secure storage. My original 'how to use it' idea:

Experiments are currently under way to determine the ‘speed’ at which the footage is broadcast to SCP-1552-1; if it exceeds lightspeed, it may be possible to employ SCP-1552 as an early warning system in case of the facility coming under attack or sustaining a containment breach (further research will be needed to determine whether such a warning would violate causality).

Regardless of this, SCP-1552-1 remains an important crisis management tool for Sector-25; although it appears impossible to impose a surface between SCP-1552-1’s ‘camera’ and SCP-1552’s face, SCP-1552’s head does not normally fill the whole image broadcast to SCP-1552-1 and useful text-based information could be readily broadcast from behind the animal (the optimum point being behind SCP-1552’s shoulders). Contingency plans have been drafted to dispatch samples of SCP-1552-1 to all other Foundation facilities; at present it appears the broadcast capacity of SCP-1552 is one-way, but should Sector-25 suffer a major incident that precludes contact with other facilities by conventional means, SCP-1552 could provide invaluable, as thus far the feed shown on SCP-1552-1 has shown itself to be completely unblockable with no range limitation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! I will. The guide I was thinking of does list 'termination' of D-class as a 'good' example, so it might just be murderdeath in general that 'terminate' was being overused for.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks - any idea how they could be improved? There's just one capital-i Incident per se at the minute, though they did revise containment procedures significantly after learning that liquids can become 1552-1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I may have missed discussions on the issue, and I am just one user, so if you get shit about it you should change it back. However, as a whole, though I'm not sure if it needs to be in there, it's much better. I'm going to give it an upvote, but I would suggest continuing to work on it as you get more feedback.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really? I thought that was verba non grata at the minute. Replaced it - as mentioned below I've just replaced it with a more Professorial note about the consequences for the incident's participants.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Replacing the term "executed" with "terminated" would probably be an improvement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Rant be-gone! I've replaced Professor Censored cursing out his underlings with a flat summary of the consequences for the incident's participants, which feels creepier anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a very original concept and nicely written. I don't know about those incident reports though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very creative idea. I think it needs a little more work. For instance, the bit about using the -1's to monitor the site felt like it should be in the containment procedures or an addendum, rather than the description. I like this, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've nuked the whole experiment log and moved the relevant bits (basically just the new restrictions on liquids near 1552) into the main article. Unfortunately I moved the rant over as well (with the obvious tweak as suggested). Is there any way we can preserve the reveal of the human outcome of the incident in a way that doesn't come across as OOC?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm of the opinion that the experiment log doesn't add anything- there's nothing really counterintuitive to the concept that needs to be addressed in it. However, YMMV on whether you want to keep it. The part that I'm absolutely sure of is that the professor's commentary needs to be cut out posthaste. There's no reason at all to include incompetent behavior on the part of the Foundation and someone ranting about it in language that's lifted out of an internet forum.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A couple of people on the forums and here have now suggested removing the experiment log. I thought it was characterful and helped suspension of disbelief by exploring how they had established 1552-1's known traits. However, I'm definitely game for removing it if people think it works better without:

Experiment-1 is probably unneeded, though it does counter fridge logic re why just the eyes are affected.
Experiment-2 is spelt out in the article, so could probably stand this or the reference in the article removing.
Experiment-3 is again establishing why researchers know something in the main article; could go if this wasn't a big deal
Experiment-4 explains the rationale for several containment measures but is mostly addressed in Addendum-01.
Experiment-5 explains the rationale for painting solid 1552-1 pieces black, but again; maybe it could be removed?
Experiment-6's main appeal is an overblown rant which has been criticised here for tone. Maybe a more subtle version could be inserted into the main entry?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Most instances of lolfoundation are from older articles. It's frowned upon in new ones.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll definitely tone it down if people think it's over-the-top, though I'm sure I've seen worse :( Was that the lolfoundation moment, or the execution of contaminated D-class/removal of contaminated body parts from staff? I did ask about that over on the forums but was told it wasn't out of line.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agree. I like the concept, but not the execution.

I'm also not a huge fan of the bulldog image—it's too cute.

Vote withheld for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: True enough. That doesn't make it a good idea to add it in.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This isn't the first time a foundation scientist has appended a note to test logs cussing out destructive or moronic behavior.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I kept going back and forth on whether I was going to upvote this. The idea of the world's oceans turning into a giant reflection of a bulldog was so weird that I almost upvoted on that alone. And then I hit the Experiment Log, which in addition to being generally unnecessary had a lolfoundation moment so bad that I could almost hear the screech of tires as I read.

No one is going to use the term "dickbaggery" in a professional document. Especially not a Professor.

You'll want to wait until you get other feedback, but I would remove the log and do a once-over for tone issues and other cleanup. As it is, I can't upvote.
